past_medical_history: Seizure disorder diagnosed at age 25, well-controlled on levetiracetam for the past 6 years
Ventriculoperitoneal shunt placed 8 months ago for presumed hydrocephalus at an outside hospital
Presumptive diagnosis of neurosarcoidosis made 6 months ago at outside hospital, treated with prednisone with initial symptomatic improvement
No history of pulmonary disease or systemic autoimmune conditions
Remote concussion from a motor vehicle accident at age 19, no residual deficits

medications.indication: Gastric protection with chronic steroid use
Osteoporosis prevention with chronic steroid use

family_history: Father diagnosed with sarcoidosis at age 52, affecting lungs and skin
Maternal uncle died of lung cancer at age 64
No family history of brain tumors or neurological malignancies

social_history.occupation: Warehouse supervisor, currently on medical leave
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, quit 3 years ago, 4 pack-year history
social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 2-3 beers per week, currently abstaining
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and two young children

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but intermittently confused. Oriented to person and place but not date. Speech with mild dysarthria. Naming intact, repetition intact, comprehension intact for simple commands but impaired for multi-step instructions. Short-term recall 1/3 at 5 minutes.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN I: not tested. CN II: visual acuity 20/25 OD, 20/30 OS. Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam limited by left pupil nonreactivity. CN III/IV/VI: left pupil fixed and dilated at 5 mm, right pupil 3 mm reactive. Nystagmus on primary gaze of the left eye. Bilateral horizontal nystagmus on lateral gaze. Extraocular movements otherwise full. CN V: decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick over the right V2 and V3 distributions. CN VII: face symmetric at rest and with activation. CN VIII: hearing grossly intact bilaterally. CN IX/X: palate elevates symmetrically, gag reflex present. CN XI: sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: tongue midline, no fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Upper extremities: strength 5/5 bilaterally in deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, and grip. Mild action tremor bilateral hands, left greater than right. Lower extremities: strength 4/5 bilateral hip flexors, 4+/5 knee extensors, 4/5 ankle dorsiflexors bilaterally. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact in upper extremities. Decreased proprioception at bilateral great toes. Vibration sense decreased at bilateral ankles.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally. Brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar reflexes 3+ bilaterally. Achilles reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses extensor bilaterally (Babinski sign positive).
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing with mild bilateral dysmetria, left greater than right. Heel-to-shin testing impaired bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements mildly slowed bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Unsteady, wide-based gait. Unable to perform tandem walking. Romberg positive.
neurological_exam.additional: VP shunt palpable along the right posterior parietal scalp with no signs of shunt malfunction. Mild papilledema noted on right fundoscopic exam. Cushingoid features noted including moon facies and central adiposity consistent with chronic steroid use.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive altered mental status, worsening lower extremity weakness, and recurrent nausea and vomiting over the past 6 months despite treatment for presumed neurosarcoidosis.
patient.history_present_illness: A 31-year-old right-handed Hispanic man presents for neurosurgical evaluation with a 6-month history of progressive cognitive decline, worsening bilateral lower extremity weakness, persistent nausea and vomiting, and recurrent headaches with intermittent dizziness. The patient reports that his symptoms began gradually approximately 6 years ago with intermittent headaches and a single unprovoked generalized tonic-clonic seizure at age 25, which led to initiation of levetiracetam. He was seizure-free for approximately 5 years on this regimen. Over the past year, however, he has experienced an accelerating decline in function. He initially developed progressive difficulty with balance, reporting multiple falls at work and at home, and his wife noted him becoming increasingly forgetful and confused. He also developed numbness and tingling over the right side of his face. Eight months ago, he was evaluated at an outside hospital where hydrocephalus was diagnosed, and a ventriculoperitoneal shunt was placed. Despite initial mild improvement following the procedure, his symptoms continued to worsen. Six months ago, an outside neurologist made a presumptive diagnosis of neurosarcoidosis based on imaging findings and mildly elevated inflammatory markers, and he was started on oral prednisone at 60 mg daily with a planned slow taper. He experienced some symptomatic improvement in the first two months, particularly with headache and nausea, but over the subsequent four months his mental status has progressively deteriorated, his lower extremity weakness has worsened to the point that he now requires a walker, and he has lost approximately 15 pounds. His wife also describes new episodes of confusion where he does not recognize familiar surroundings and has difficulty following conversations. He denies fevers, night sweats, skin rashes, joint pains, or respiratory symptoms. He has no history of tuberculosis exposure or travel to endemic areas. His father was diagnosed with sarcoidosis, which initially supported the working diagnosis.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7-8 Hz alpha, poorly organized, low amplitude
background.symmetry: Mild asymmetry with lower amplitudes over the right temporal region
background.reactivity: Attenuated but poorly reactive to eye opening

artifacts.description: Intermittent, does not obscure underlying activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient's elevated intracranial pressure concerns
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photoparoxysmal response. No photic driving.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating diffuse background slowing in the theta range with superimposed intermittent focal slowing in the right temporal region and left posterior quadrant. No definite epileptiform discharges identified during this recording. The pattern could be seen in diffuse meningeal disease, multifocal structural lesions, metabolic encephalopathy, or medication effects. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording. No sleep stages captured. Hyperventilation not performed. Continuous EEG monitoring may reveal additional findings.

signal_characteristics.T1_pre_contrast: Heterogeneous, predominantly hypointense solid component with hypointense cystic component
signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Heterogeneous enhancement of solid component; cystic component does not enhance
signal_characteristics.T2: Cystic component extremely hyperintense; solid component heterogeneously hyperintense
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Cystic component suppresses on FLAIR; surrounding FLAIR hyperintensity consistent with edema or infiltrative change

findings.mass_effect: Mild mass effect on the right lateral ventricle temporal horn. No significant midline shift.
findings.borders: Irregular and poorly defined margins of the solid component, suggesting infiltrative process

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Solid enhancing component with adjacent non-enhancing cyst

findings.mass_effect: Mild compression of the ventral pons
findings.borders: Well-circumscribed enhancing nodule with associated cyst

findings.location: Left cerebellar hemisphere extending across the midline
findings.mass_effect: Mild compression of the fourth ventricle without frank obstructive hydrocephalus
findings.borders: Relatively well-defined but with irregular margins

findings.location: Bilateral basal meninges, extending along the skull base from the anterior cranial fossa to the posterior fossa

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Extensive nodular and plaque-like enhancement along the basal meninges

findings.borders: Diffuse, irregular thickening without discrete mass

additional_observations: VP shunt catheter in situ with tip in the right lateral ventricle; ventricles mildly prominent but no acute hydrocephalus
No hemorrhagic components identified on susceptibility-weighted imaging
No abnormal leptomeningeal enhancement noted in the spinal canal on available cervical sequences
mri.impression: Multifocal solid and cystic intracranial lesions involving the right temporal lobe, prepontine cistern, and left cerebellum, with extensive diffuse nodular basimeningeal enhancement. The differential diagnosis includes neurosarcoidosis (given the known clinical history and basimeningeal pattern), CNS lymphoma, tuberculosis or other chronic granulomatous infection, meningeal carcinomatosis, and less likely a diffuse infiltrative neoplasm such as gliomatosis. The cystic components with FLAIR suppression and extreme T2 hyperintensity are somewhat atypical for all entities in the differential. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: No evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy
ecg.interpretation: Normal ECG. No acute or chronic abnormalities identified.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 12.8 x10^9/L (H), Neutrophils 78 % (H), Lymphocytes 14 % (L), Glucose 148 mg/dL (H), ESR 38 mm/hr (H), CRP 14.2 mg/L (H), Albumin 3.4 g/dL (L).

findings.type: MR Spectroscopy — single voxel from right temporal lobe solid component
findings.location: Right temporal lobe mass, solid enhancing portion

signal_characteristics.choline: Markedly elevated choline peak (Cho/Cr ratio 2.8, normal <1.5)
signal_characteristics.lipid_lactate: Elevated lipid and lactate peaks suggesting necrosis or anaerobic metabolism
signal_characteristics.myoinositol: Mildly elevated myoinositol, which can be seen in both gliomas and granulomatous disease

output.impression: The mildly elevated myoinositol is nonspecific. Overall, these findings strongly favor a neoplastic etiology over neurosarcoidosis. Clinical correlation recommended.

signal_characteristics.rCBV: Moderately elevated rCBV (ratio 2.1 relative to contralateral normal-appearing white matter) in the solid enhancing component
signal_characteristics.rCBF: Elevated regional cerebral blood flow in the enhancing rim
signal_characteristics.permeability: Increased Ktrans values suggesting blood-brain barrier breakdown

output.impression: Perfusion imaging demonstrates moderately elevated rCBV in the solid enhancing components of the right temporal and left cerebellar masses. The elevated permeability metrics support blood-brain barrier disruption. Clinical correlation recommended.

Previous_Biopsy_Review.test: Original biopsy review (right temporal lobe, 6 months ago)
Previous_Biopsy_Review.value: Non-diagnostic — predominantly reactive gliosis and normal dura without evidence of neoplasm, granuloma, or infection
Acellular proteinaceous fluid, negative for malignant cells, no inflammatory infiltrate

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Histological diagnosis Astrocytoma with protoplasmic features  (H), WHO Grade Grade 2 (with features of higher-grade behavior)  (H), Ki-67 proliferation index 8-12%  (H), IDH1 R132H mutation Positive  (H), ATRX Lost (absent nuclear staining)  (H), MGMT promoter methylation Methylated  (H), p53 immunostain Strongly positive (>50% nuclei)  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Ki-67 elevated at 8-12% for grade 2 tumor
Previous biopsy was false-negative due to sampling error

CT_Chest_Abdomen_Pelvis.value: Clear bilateral lung fields. No hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy. No pulmonary nodules or masses.
Liver, spleen, kidneys, pancreas, and adrenal glands are normal. No lymphadenopathy. VP shunt tubing noted in the peritoneal cavity.
No lytic or blastic lesions identified.

signal_characteristics.brocas_area: Robust activation in left inferior frontal gyrus (Broca's area), well-separated from the right temporal lobe mass
signal_characteristics.wernickes_area: Activation in left posterior superior temporal gyrus (Wernicke's area), no involvement by tumor
signal_characteristics.laterality_index: Left hemisphere dominant for language (laterality index 0.82)

signal_characteristics.right_hand: Left precentral gyrus motor activation, no tumor involvement
signal_characteristics.left_hand: Right precentral gyrus motor activation, adjacent to but not involved by tumor
signal_characteristics.bilateral_foot: Activation at vertex bilaterally, no tumor involvement

output.impression: fMRI language and motor mapping demonstrates left hemisphere language dominance. The right precentral motor cortex for left hand function is adjacent to but not directly involved by tumor. Surgical approach should aim to preserve the corticospinal tract on the right side. The cerebellar mass proximity to the dentate nucleus and cerebellar peduncles poses a separate surgical risk for worsening ataxia. Clinical correlation recommended.

results.title: Protoplasmic astrocytomas: a review of clinical, radiological and pathological features
results.key_finding: Protoplasmic astrocytomas are rare WHO grade II tumors with distinctive histological features including low cellularity and prominent mucinous microcystic change. They can mimic non-neoplastic lesions on imaging and biopsy, leading to diagnostic delay.

results.title: Leptomeningeal dissemination of low-grade gliomas: systematic review
results.key_finding: Leptomeningeal dissemination occurs in approximately 3-5% of diffuse gliomas, more commonly in high-grade tumors but occasionally in IDH-mutant low-grade astrocytomas. Prognosis with meningeal spread is significantly worse than for localized disease.

results.title: IDH-mutant astrocytomas: molecular classification and treatment implications
results.key_finding: IDH-mutant astrocytomas benefit from maximal safe resection followed by radiation therapy and temozolomide chemotherapy. MGMT methylation predicts better response to temozolomide. Even in the setting of disseminated disease, temozolomide with radiation offers median survival improvements.

output.summary: Protoplasmic astrocytomas are rare WHO grade II diffuse astrocytomas characterized by low cellularity, mucinous microcystic matrix, and bland cytology that can make them extremely difficult to diagnose on small biopsies. They may be mistaken for reactive gliosis or non-neoplastic conditions. Leptomeningeal dissemination, while uncommon in low-grade gliomas (3-5%), carries a significantly worse prognosis. For IDH-mutant astrocytomas with dissemination, treatment typically involves maximal safe resection of the dominant mass(es) followed by craniospinal radiation and temozolomide chemotherapy. MGMT methylation (present in this case) predicts better chemotherapy response.

output.proposed: Temozolomide 150 mg/m2 for 5 days of 28-day cycle (adjuvant chemotherapy)
interactions: Levetiracetam: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction. Levetiracetam is a preferred antiepileptic in glioma patients as it does not induce hepatic enzymes.
Prednisone/dexamethasone: Concurrent corticosteroids are commonly used during glioma treatment for edema management. No direct drug interaction but may mask symptoms of tumor progression.
contraindications: Severe myelosuppression: Temozolomide causes dose-limiting thrombocytopenia and neutropenia. Baseline CBC must be checked before each cycle.
warnings: Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) is recommended during concurrent temozolomide and radiation due to lymphopenia risk
Monitor CBC weekly during first cycle, then prior to each subsequent cycle
Hepatotoxicity: Monitor liver function tests periodically
Temozolomide bioavailability is reduced by food — administer on empty stomach
output.formulary_status: On formulary, requires neuro-oncology approval for initiation
alternatives: PCV regimen (procarbazine, CCNU/lomustine, vincristine) — alternative combination chemotherapy for IDH-mutant gliomas
Lomustine monotherapy — used in recurrent gliomas
Bevacizumab — anti-VEGF therapy for recurrent high-grade gliomas, less evidence in low-grade setting

findings.location: Cervical, thoracic, and lumbar spinal cord — diffuse involvement

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Multiple small enhancing nodules coating the surface of the spinal cord and cauda equina nerve roots throughout the entire spinal canal
signal_characteristics.T2: Mild T2 hyperintensity of the spinal cord at C3-C5 and T8-T10 levels, possibly representing edema or tumor infiltration

findings.mass_effect: No significant cord compression. Mild expansion of the cord at C4 level.
findings.borders: Diffuse coating pattern rather than discrete masses

findings.location: L2 level, posterior to the conus medullaris

output.impression: Diffuse nodular leptomeningeal enhancement throughout the entire spinal canal, coating the spinal cord surface and cauda equina nerve roots, with a discrete 8 mm enhancing nodule at L2. In the context of a confirmed astrocytoma diagnosis, this represents leptomeningeal gliomatosis — a rare but well-described pattern of CNS dissemination. The extent of spinal involvement would necessitate craniospinal radiation rather than focal radiation therapy. Clinical correlation recommended.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Astrocytoma with protoplasmic features (IDH-mutant, diffuse) with leptomeningeal dissemination

differential.key_distinguishing: Diffuse basimeningeal enhancement pattern is classic for sarcoidosis; however, absence of pulmonary involvement on chest CT, negative initial biopsy for granulomata, and progressive disease despite corticosteroid therapy argue against this diagnosis

Multiple enhancing lesions with meningeal involvement can be seen in CNS lymphoma; typically more homogeneous enhancement, periventricular predominance, and resolves with steroids (the partial steroid response here was misleading)
Nodular meningeal enhancement can mimic tuberculous meningitis; however, no exposure history, no endemic travel, and cystic components are atypical for TB
Multiple intracranial lesions of varying sizes could suggest metastatic disease; however, young age, no known primary, and negative body CT argue strongly against this
Can mimic diffuse meningeal disease and cause cranial neuropathies; however, absence of sinus, pulmonary, or renal involvement makes this unlikely
Diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement with parenchymal masses; no primary cancer identified on systemic workup

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without contrast to characterize the multifocal intracranial lesions and meningeal enhancement pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multifocal solid and cystic masses in right temporal lobe, prepontine cistern, and left cerebellum with diffuse nodular basimeningeal enhancement

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive laboratory panel including CBC, BMP, inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), ACE level, and LDH
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated ESR and CRP; borderline ACE level; steroid-induced leukocytosis and hyperglycemia; normal LDH

optimal_actions.action: Perform EEG given history of seizure disorder and altered mental status
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Diffuse slowing with multifocal areas of intermittent focal slowing; no epileptiform discharges

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to evaluate for systemic sarcoidosis and rule out primary malignancy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No hilar lymphadenopathy, no pulmonary sarcoidosis, no primary malignancy or metastases

optimal_actions.action: Request MR spectroscopy of the dominant right temporal lobe mass to differentiate neoplasm from granulomatous disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated choline, reduced NAA, lipid/lactate peaks — metabolite profile consistent with neoplasm rather than granulomatous disease

optimal_actions.action: Perform repeat stereotactic biopsy targeting the most enhancing solid component, with sufficient tissue for molecular profiling
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Astrocytoma with protoplasmic features, IDH1-mutant, ATRX loss, MGMT methylated, elevated Ki-67

optimal_actions.action: Order complete spinal MRI with contrast to evaluate for leptomeningeal dissemination
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Diffuse nodular leptomeningeal enhancement throughout the entire spinal canal

optimal_actions.action: Present at multidisciplinary neuro-oncology tumor board for treatment planning
Check drug interactions for planned temozolomide chemotherapy with current medications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant interaction with levetiracetam; PJP prophylaxis recommended with concurrent temozolomide and radiation

optimal_actions.action: Perform fMRI for pre-surgical language and motor mapping
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left hemisphere language dominance; right temporal mass in non-dominant hemisphere; motor cortex adjacent but not invaded

critical_actions: Recognize that progressive disease despite corticosteroid therapy for presumed neurosarcoidosis warrants reconsideration of the diagnosis
Order repeat biopsy with adequate tissue sampling after initial non-diagnostic biopsy, targeting the most enhancing solid component
Obtain MR spectroscopy to help differentiate neoplasm from granulomatous disease before repeat biopsy
Evaluate for systemic sarcoidosis with chest CT (absence of pulmonary involvement argues against neurosarcoidosis)
Order complete spinal MRI to assess the full extent of leptomeningeal dissemination for treatment planning
Refer to neuro-oncology tumor board for multidisciplinary treatment planning given the complexity of disseminated disease
contraindicated_actions: Continuing corticosteroid monotherapy without re-evaluation of the underlying diagnosis, as this delays appropriate oncological treatment
Accepting the initial non-diagnostic biopsy as definitive evidence against neoplasm without repeat tissue sampling
Administering whole-brain radiation without first establishing tissue diagnosis, as this could mask lymphoma and prevent accurate staging
Starting empiric anti-tuberculosis therapy without supporting evidence (no exposure, no endemic travel, atypical imaging features)
Performing aggressive surgical resection without pre-operative functional mapping, given proximity of one lesion to motor cortex
key_reasoning_points: Neurosarcoidosis was a reasonable initial presumptive diagnosis given the diffuse basimeningeal enhancement pattern and mildly elevated inflammatory markers, but progressive disease despite corticosteroid therapy should have prompted earlier diagnostic re-evaluation
The absence of pulmonary sarcoidosis on chest CT (present in >90% of sarcoidosis cases) is a significant negative finding that argues against the neurosarcoidosis diagnosis
A family history of sarcoidosis (father) served as a red herring that reinforced the incorrect initial diagnosis

The initial biopsy was non-diagnostic due to sampling error — protoplasmic astrocytomas are notoriously difficult to diagnose on small biopsies because of their low cellularity and bland cytology that mimics reactive gliosis

The initial symptomatic improvement on corticosteroids was misleading — steroids reduce peritumoral edema in gliomas and can cause partial tumor response, mimicking treatment response for an inflammatory condition
Protoplasmic astrocytomas are rare tumors that can present with diffuse leptomeningeal dissemination, mimicking inflammatory or infectious meningeal disease on imaging
MR spectroscopy was the critical differentiating tool: elevated choline and reduced NAA pointed to neoplasm rather than granulomatous disease, guiding the decision for repeat biopsy

Despite the histologically low-grade appearance (WHO grade 2), the clinical behavior with leptomeningeal dissemination and elevated Ki-67 index suggests more aggressive biology requiring combined-modality treatment
Molecular profiling (IDH mutation, MGMT methylation) is essential for guiding treatment decisions even in the setting of disseminated disease

metadata.condition_name: Brain tumor — high-grade glioma (astrocytoma with protoplasmic features)
metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle due to initial misdiagnosis as neurosarcoidosis, false-negative initial biopsy, partial steroid response mimicking treatment success, family history of sarcoidosis as red herring, and rare tumor subtype with atypical leptomeningeal dissemination pattern
